ground_truth:
1) **Intake / request (Requester → Applied ML Onboarding owns intake):** Requester (Product/Solutions Eng) files a Linear *ENG* ticket labeled `model-onboarding` containing model identity (name/version, type, source URL, tokenizer/special tokens), proposed catalog surface (Redwood model ID, endpoint compatibility, aliases), license/redistribution terms, safety/policy concerns, and target workloads/regions/SLOs; the **Linear ticket is the intake summary (SSoT artifact)**.  

2) **Intake gates (must pass before engineering starts):**  
- **License pre-check** - *Owner:* Finance-Legal liaison + **Security**; *Outcome:* approved / approved-with-restrictions / blocked; *Artifact:* **link to Security risk review entry**.  
- **Baseline evaluation plan** - *Owner:* **Applied ML**; *Artifact:* eval plan recorded on the Linear ticket + **eval harness run link(s)** (plan must include ≥1 internal prompt set + ≥1 public benchmark).  
- **Feasibility / runtime fit** - *Owner:* **Serving Runtime**; *Artifact:* feasibility note + any required runtime flags (tokenizer/KV cache/kernel coverage).  
A model is **"ready for implementation"** only when license is approved (restrictions documented), eval plan meets the minimum, Runtime reports no blockers, and a **rollout owner is named (Release Eng + Platform)**.

3) **Security & compliance risk review (required before rollout; Owner: Security & Compliance):** Perform provenance/integrity (record upstream tag/commit + **file hashes**), supply-chain scanning (containers/build env + malware scan), license/acceptable-use confirmation (and restriction text for docs/terms), privacy/data-handling validation, and abuse/policy alignment; *Artifacts:* **risk review entry with decision** (`approved`/`approved-with-restrictions`/`blocked`) and any **exception record** (if needed, requires VP Eng approval) linked in both the Linear ticket and the registry PR.

4) **Quantization profiles (required for catalog eligibility):** Build and validate profiles **fp16**, **int8-weight-only**, and **int4-weight-only** (or document N/A with Runtime signoff).  
- **Correctness smoke** - *Owner:* Serving Runtime; *Artifacts:* tokenizer round-trip + determinism checks.  
- **Quality delta check** - *Owner:* Applied ML; *Artifact:* **Suite A eval harness runs** on fp16 and default profile; must have no `p0` regressions and win-rate delta vs fp16 ≥ -5%.  
- **Performance check** - *Owner:* Serving Runtime; *Artifact:* **perf suite run IDs** showing the default profile meets the performance bar.  
Additional required artifacts: **quant build logs** (Drive) + the profile list/default indicated in the **model registry PR** (exceptions labeled `quant-exception` on the Linear ticket).

5) **Evaluation gates on the candidate build (Owner: Applied ML; required signoff: Applied ML + Product):** Run and log in the eval harness:  
- **Suite A (internal regression)** with `prompt-sets/hosted-api/core-regression` (no `p0` regressions; ≥50% win-rate vs baseline).  
- **Suite B (public benchmark sanity)** (no task drops >5% absolute vs expected/adjacent baselines; provide score table + notes).  
- **Suite C (safety/policy screening)** (no increase in disallowed content; refusal templates compatible; include policy config notes).  
- **Suite D (tool/function calling)** only if marketed for it (JSON schema conformance ≥99%; include validation report).  
*Launch packet artifacts (required):* **all eval run URLs**, **one-paragraph risk assessment**, **recommended default inference config**, and **known limitations** (exceptions labeled `eval-exception`; any `p0` failure blocks ship).

6) **Performance bar / reliability gate (Standard owner: Serving Runtime; enforcement: Release Eng + SRE):** Run `perf-canary` on **H100-80GB** + one secondary class, contexts 2k/8k/16k, concurrency 1/8/32, streaming on; must meet TTFT/throughput/stability/error-rate thresholds and KV-leak check.  
*Artifacts:* **benchmark run IDs**, **raw logs**, and **summary table** (stored on the Performance Standards page and linked from the Linear ticket).  
*Required signoffs (recorded on the Linear intake ticket):* **Serving Runtime** (perf/stability), **SRE** (capacity/readiness), **Release Engineering** (rollout plan present).

7) **Rollout to Hosted API catalog (Runbook; Rollout owner: Release Eng; Routing owner: Platform; On-call: runtime+platform):** Preconditions: Linear ticket in `ready-for-rollout`, eval gates passed + risk paragraph present, performance bar signed off for default profile, and Security risk review approved/exception linked.  
*Required rollout artifacts:* **GitHub PR in `redwood-model-registry`** (model ID/version/tokenizer, context limits, features, quant profiles/default, and **artifact hashes/provenance notes**), **perf run IDs**, **eval links**, and **Security approval + restriction text**.  
Procedure: (1) merge registry PR only after approvals; (2) **dark launch** behind `router-policy` feature flag (`disabled`, `internal`) and run staging smoke tests (streaming chat completions, JSON schema output, max-context overflow behavior); (3) **prod canary** 0.5% in us-east-1 for 30-60 min monitoring `hosted_inference_latency`, `hosted_inference_errors`, `gpu_oom_events`, `quality_regression_alerts` (abort/rollback if error rate >0.5% or TTFT p95 SLO breach for 10 min); (4) expand to 5% then add regions; configure required **fallback policy** (previous stable in-family, then cross-family safe default) and keep the old model as fallback ≥7 days if replacing a default/alias; (5) at 100% intended routing, mark Linear ticket `shipped` and **DevEx/Docs** publishes docs (model ID, defaults, limitations, and any restriction text).

8) **Post-launch monitoring (first 7 days; required):** Keep canary/quality shadowing enabled where possible (including the **1% quality canary/shadow-to-baseline**), watch `quality_regression_alerts` and error-budget burn plus latency/error/OOM dashboards, and file a follow-up ticket if thresholds trip; rollback triggers include sustained TTFT p95 regression >20% (30 min), 5xx >0.5% (10 min), or confirmed `p0` quality regression, with rollback executed by disabling routing, promoting fallback, and logging/incident comms per the runbook.

ground_truth: A production rollout plan for a new LLM model version on **Redwood Inference (Hosted, Dedicated, and Private)** must be a published plan (linked from the change ticket, using the canonical template) that includes: **(1) Summary** (what/why/expected impact and on-point owner + Slack), **(2) Scope** (Hosted/Dedicated/Private, regions, request types, customer tiers/allowlists), **(3) Versioning & compatibility** (alias, new immutable version id, backward-compat notes for tool-calling/format/limits, max context/output tokens) and links to **READY_FOR_PROD readiness artifacts** (functional compatibility, performance profile, reliability/failure modes, quality baseline, operational ownership/oncall + dashboards), **(4) Success criteria/guardrails with an explicit pass/fail threshold table and windows** using (or stricter than) the required SLOs: 5xx rate pass **≤ baseline +0.05% over 30m** / fail **> baseline +0.20% over 15m**; timeout rate pass **≤ baseline +0.03% over 30m** / fail **> baseline +0.10% over 15m**; admission rejects pass **≤0.5% over 30m** / fail **>2.0% over 10m**; p95 latency (non-streaming) pass **≤ +5% vs baseline over 30m** / fail **> +10% over 15m**; TTFT (streaming) pass **≤ +10% over 30m** / fail **> +25% over 15m**; long-tail fail **p99 > 2.5× baseline for 10m**; capacity fail **GPU saturation >92% for 15m with rising queue time**; tokens/sec/GPU pass **≥ baseline −3% over 60m** / fail **< baseline −8% over 30m**; cache hit rate pass **within −5% abs over 60m** / fail **within −15% abs over 30m**; quality pass **eval ≥ baseline −0.3** / fail **< baseline −0.7**, plus fail if **2+ reproducible customer quality reports within 24h** (canary/ramp decision windows are **≥30m**, full validation **24h after 100%**), **(5) Monitoring plan** (dashboard links, trace/log queries, alert list and owners) and enabling the **rollout_slo_guard** alert bundle (paged SRE on any fail threshold; notify platform/runtime channels), **(6) Rollout steps/strategy** (canary and ramp schedule with decision points recorded; recommended 0.5%→5%→25%→50%→100% with minimum observation windows; blast-radius controls such as per-region caps, max traffic %, allowlists, concurrency caps; A/B plan if used), **(7) Rollback & fallback rules** that map each fail threshold to an action and explicitly define Router policy requirements-**primary version id, ordered fallback chain (must include prior default when making a new default), health-check gates for automatic fallback (5xx/timeouts/saturation), blast-radius controls, and the rollback target policy id**-plus what happens to in-flight/streaming requests; rollback procedure is restoring the prior policy, setting new version traffic to 0%, and confirming recovery within ~15 minutes (escalate if not), and **automatic fallback must trigger** on sustained fail-threshold 5xx/timeouts/admission rejects (noting fallback can mask quality, so quality guardrails still apply), **(8) Customer communications plan** (who is notified, when, channels, and required message content; links to support tickets/emails) following: always notify **Dedicated customers** getting a new default and **all impacted Private customers**; notify Hosted customers for material behavior changes (format/tool-calling/limits/latency/reliability/safety); timing is **Dedicated ≥5 business days**, **Private per contract (default 10 business days)**, **Hosted ≥72h** for material changes (otherwise release notes); messages must include what/why/when (TZ/duration)/best- & worst-case impact/opt-out or pinning path/support contact/rollback posture, and comms must be reviewed by **Customer Success owner + Inference PM** (plus **Security** for regulated Private), and **(9) Approvals & change window** including change class (1-3), approvers and timestamps, and approved window (Hosted/Dedicated prefer Tue-Thu 09:00-14:00 PT; Private must match customer window): Class 1 requires **Oncall Eng Manager or Platform Eng TL**; Class 2 requires **Platform Eng Manager + SRE oncall delegate + Inference PM**; Class 3 requires **VP Eng (or delegate) + Product Director+ and Security if compliance-impacting**; additional mandatory approvers when triggered include **Solutions Eng lead + Private Deployments Eng TL** (any Private), **Customer Success Director** (SLA/contract impact), and **Security & Compliance** (data residency/retention impact). For **Private** rollouts, the plan must also include customer-approved maintenance windows (local time, approver, downtime requirement), current pinning/opt-out and how long the prior version stays available (**≥30 days unless contract says otherwise**), confirmation rollback artifacts are pre-staged and verified, data residency/retention/telemetry changes, network/identity/RBAC dependencies with customer approval, and customer-specific post-upgrade validation; rollback must occur if fail thresholds hit during the window or if the customer requests it. Finally, the plan must specify **status page/incident comms** triggers (e.g., widespread Hosted impact >15m, Private impact outside window, or rollback after >25% ramp/after notifications) and commit to post-rollout validation and documentation (including outcome notes within 48h).

   - A prescriptive checklist, gating criteria, and automated tests required to safely introduce runtime plugins into Redwood's inference runtime.

3. **Rollout/rollback Steps**:
   - The process should be documented in a manual that includes architecture rationale, kernel scheduling implications, profiling playbook steps, benchmarking commands, an acceptance gate checklist, and example release-note scaffolding.
   - For Hosted + Dedicated deployments, the change triggers an incident or wide impact, use status page rules.

4. **Mandatory Customer Communications**:
   - All customer communications MUST include:
     * What is changing (model alias and new version id if customer pins versions).
     * Why we are changing (benefit statement).
     * When (date/time + timezone + duration).
     * Impact assessment (best-case and worst-case).
     * How to opt out or pin a version.
     * How to get help (support channel and escalation path).
     * Rollback posture (we can roll back during window if thresholds violated).

5. **Channels**:
   - Dedicated/Private: email to customer technical contacts + a support ticket for tracking.
   - Hosted: release notes; optionally email to top impacted orgs.

6. **Security Review**:
   - For Private customers in regulated environments, also require Security review.

7. **Templates (snippets)**:
   - A summary of the manual describing the end-to-end process and operational discipline used for changing inference fidelity inside the runtime: any deliberate change that affects numeric precision, kernel selection, batching policy, KV-cache warmup, model variant selection, or routing heuristics that we expect to change latency/cost/quality tradeoffs. The document combines architecture rationale, kernel scheduling implications, profiling playbook steps, benchmarking commands, an acceptance gate checklist, and example release-note scaffolding.

This process is intended for runtime engineers, kernel authors, and release owners. It applies to any code that executes inside the serving runtime process or modifies kernel scheduling behavior, including third-party kernel drivers or accelerator adapters, custom scheduler plugins and admission controllers, observability sidecars that inject telemetry or sampling hooks, experimental serialization/deserialization modules that touch request/response flows.
ground_truth:
To ship an emergency **serving-runtime hotfix** to production (Hosted + Dedicated), Redwood Inference follows this end-to-end process:

1) **Open the emergency change + record the minimum checklist** (in a Linear/Jira ticket and the incident timeline/thread): state the fix hypothesis, what's changing (code/config/infra), blast radius (Hosted regions, Dedicated clusters/customers, model families), risk assessment (security/data/perf), a **rollback plan with explicit triggers**, and who owns post-change monitoring; ensure an incident channel/thread exists with an identified IC.

2) **Get required approvals (no exceptions):** two-person approval from **(a) IC (or SRE on-call)** and **(b) the serving-runtime Service Owner (or delegate)**, recorded in PR review/incident thread/change record. Add scenario-based approvals as applicable: **Security on-call** (security/exploit), **Product on-call/PM** (customer-visible behavior changes), and **Customer Success Escalation Manager (CSEM)** when Dedicated/customer maintenance coordination is required.

3) **Prepare the hotfix PR (Serving Runtime Hotfix Runbook):** cut `hotfix/runtime-<YYYYMMDD>-<short-issue>`, cherry-pick only the minimal fix, update the Runtime Hotfixes changelog, open a PR (prefer release branch), and obtain emergency reviews from **runtime on-call + SRE on-call (or IC)**; include incident link, risk, rollback, and rollout owners.

4) **Build + supply-chain sign artifacts (mandatory):** run the approved hotfix build pipeline to produce the new runtime image in the production registry plus **SBOM** and **provenance attestation**, and **verify cosign signatures/attestations before rollout**; image signing cannot be skipped (SBOM delay is only allowed with Security on-call approval and must be attached within 4 hours).

5) **Validate before rollout (minimum required even in Sev-1):** run unit tests for the affected module, one perf canary for a representative model, and a stress test matching the incident pattern; record results in the PR confirming error-rate improvement, **no >10% p95 regression** (same workload), and no increase in OOM/GPU reset counters.

6) **Post rollout notes to the incident channel:** new runtime image tag, expected rollout impact (restarts/brief resets), the **canary target**, and **rollback criteria** (metrics + customer symptom). A canary is required unless explicitly waived by IC (with rationale recorded).

7) **Roll out to Hosted (Platform/SRE; Hosted Rollout + Rollback Procedure):** announce rollout start + tag; set `runtime_image_tag=<new-tag>` and sync via ArgoCD (`hosted-runtime`). Roll out in order **us-west-2a canary (10%) → us-west-2 (50%) → us-east-1 (50%) → eu-central-1 (50%) → global**, monitoring each step for at least 15 minutes (success rate, p95 for top routes, GPU OOM, router fallback, error-budget burn). **Rollback immediately** by reverting to the last-known-good tag and ArgoCD syncing if 5xx/stream aborts persist >5 min, OOM spikes >2× baseline, p95 regresses >20% for >10 min, crash loops appear, or customer reports correlate with rollout start; announce rollback and next steps in the incident channel.

8) **Roll out to Dedicated (Infra + CSEM; Dedicated Cluster Hotfix Rollout Notes):** collect required inputs (customer/cluster ID, current/target tags, expected restart impact, rollback tag, scope), obtain approvals (**IC/SRE + Dedicated service owner + CSEM sign-off on messaging**), and **communicate to customers** (unless IC explicitly waives due to active outage): state brief interruption risk, provide a rollout window, and describe rollback criteria in plain language. Canary one Dedicated cluster first (prefer internal dogfood/lowest-traffic with CSEM approval) and validate ~30 minutes (success rate, p95, OOM, restart frequency, customer health checks); then schedule remaining clusters, rolling **no more than 3 in parallel unless IC approves**. Per cluster: confirm comms sent, update Helm `runtime_image_tag`, ArgoCD sync `dedicated-<cluster-id>-runtime`, watch for CrashLoopBackOff, run customer smoke checks (chat/embeddings/streaming). If rollback triggers hit, revert the tag, restart if needed, and **notify the customer immediately** (CSEM).

9) **Mandatory Sev-1 customer communications (Status Page + Customer Notifications):** IC owns accuracy/cadence; CSEM owns direct enterprise/Dedicated outreach; initial acknowledgment **within 10 minutes**, updates **every 30 minutes** while impact continues, and a resolution message **within 15 minutes** of confirmed mitigation. Each update must include customer symptoms, affected surfaces (regions/clusters/models), mitigation steps, ETA with confidence level, and any workarounds; provide a post-incident summary within **1 business day** (required for enterprise).

10) **Post-change closeout:** update the incident timeline with start/change/outcome, record cluster/region outcomes, and within **2 business days** complete incident follow-up (what changed and why it worked), document any release-process deviations/corrective actions, remove temporary flags/throttles (with owner + deadline), and create a task to promote the hotfix into the next regular release.

question: Do I need approval to add a large planter or wall mounted art in a Redwood office, and what details should I include in the request?

["Title: Office Art & Plant Guidelines

Scope:
- Applies to common areas (kitchen, lounges, corridors) and individual desks in open offices.

4. Temporary & reversible: most installations should be removable without tools or wall damage.", "Conflict Resolution:

answer:
Yes, you typically do not need formal approval for adding a large planter or wall-mounted art. However, it is recommended that such requests be submitted at least 10 business days prior to ensure adequate planning and consideration of shared space norms.

The key points to include in the request are:
- A detailed description of the proposed installation
- The estimated weight of the item(s)
- The mounting method (e.g., wall mount, pedestal, etc.)
- Contact information for the owner or designated steward
- A maintenance plan that addresses how the item will be cleaned and maintained without causing hazards

If there are any concerns about the impact on shared spaces or accessibility, it is a good idea to include those in your request as well. This can help ensure that the installation aligns with Redwood's guidelines for maintaining safe and respectful shared workspaces.
ground_truth: Yes-larger items like floor-standing planters or wall-hung installations over 30 inches in any dimension require approval. Email people-ops@redwood.com with the location, dimensions, weight, and installation/mounting method (and include an owner/contact and maintenance plan as applicable).

question: How often should facilities water common-area snake plants and ZZ plants in the office?
contexts:
["Daily care checklist (facilities team):
1) Morning quick-check (mon-fri): inspect high-traffic common area plants for wilting, standing water, or pests.
2) Watering: follow the plant-specific schedule (see table below); avoid overwatering. Use saucers to prevent leaks.
3) Clean leaves monthly: wipe dust buildup to keep plants healthy and improve air quality.
4) Report issues: log major problems in #facilities with a photo and location.

Watering schedule by plant type (general guidance):
- Snake plant (Sansevieria): water every 4–6 weeks. Low light tolerant.
- ZZ plant (Zamioculcas zamiifolia): water every 4–6 weeks. Very low maintenance.
- Pothos/Devil's ivy: water every 10–14 days; trim vines if they encroach on walkways.
- Spider plant: water every 10–14 days; prefers bright, indirect light.
- Peace lily: water every 7–10 days; avoid placement where pollen may bother coworkers.
- Succulents/Cacti: water every 3–6 weeks depending on season.", "Overview:

This page documents lightweight, community-driven guidelines for caring for office plants and maintaining a pleasant desk-plant ecosystem. It is intended to keep shared spaces tidy, reduce plant mortality, and provide simple escalation paths for issues. These guidelines are informal and may be superseded by facility vendor contracts or building policies.

Scope:
- Applies to all plants in shared spaces (kitchen, conference rooms, lounge) and to potted plants kept at individual desks when they interact with shared facilities (water stations, plant shelves).
- Excludes customer office gardens and third-party contracted plant maintenance.

Principles:
1. Low-effort, resilient plants preferred: choose hardier species for communal areas (e.g., pothos, snake plant, ZZ plant).
2. Shared responsibility: facilities owns ordering and large maintenance; community volunteers do day-to-day checks.
3. Non-judgmental culture: if a plant is wilting, flag it; no shaming.", "Desk Jungle Guidelines (for personal plants):
- Pot size: keep to 4–8 inch pots for desks; larger pots belong in floor planters.
- Watering: use a tray to prevent accidental spills; water at desk only in non-sensitive equipment areas.
- Lighting: if a plant requires bright light, place near windows; avoid blocking walkways.
- Allergies: pick hypoallergenic species when possible or keep plants contained inside cubicle boundaries.

Shared Planters & Conference Rooms:
- Labeling: all shared planter pots should have a small waterproof label with plant name and last-watering date.
- Rotation: every three months facilities will rotate plants to refresh displays and prevent one area bearing all sun/heat exposure.", "Placement and safety guidelines:
- Do not place plants where they block egress, fire extinguishers, electrical panels, or sensors.
- Avoid hanging plants directly over desks where drips could occur.
- Ensure pots have trays; mopping and carpet damage incidents have previously come from unchecked overflow.
- Do not place heavily-shedding plants in meeting rooms or near shared keyboards and equipment.
- Plants with known mild-to-high toxicity (e.g., philodendron, oleander) are not allowed in common areas.

Personal desk plants (employee-owned):
- Allowed but must be maintained by the owner. If a plant becomes a nuisance (pests, mold, water damage) facilities may request removal.
- Use a waterproof tray and keep a small saucer under the pot.
- Notify facilities in case of spider/ant/pest sightings so coordinated treatment can occur.", "Overview:

This page documents the recommended care, placement, and procurement guidelines for office plants and low-effort decor in Redwood Inference offices. The goal is to maintain a safe, healthy workplace, reduce allergens and pest risk, and ensure plants support wellbeing and culture without creating maintenance burden.

Scope:

Quick decibel sampling procedure", "## Guidance: how to choose min/max (recommendation playbook)
Use this section for tickets like “How should we set bounds to meet our SLA?”

### Step 1: Determine objective constraints
Ask:
- What is the target p95 latency and error rate during peak?
- What is the peak window duration and how fast does it ramp?
- What is the maximum monthly spend or peak-day cap?
- Is the application allowed to degrade gracefully at peak (fallback model, rate limiting, shedding)?

### Step 2: Recommend a starting min
Principle: **Min should cover the first 5–15 minutes of the expected ramp** (or the time it takes reactive scaling to catch up), not the entire peak.

Heuristics (starting points):
- If they have a predictable daily ramp and strict SLOs: set min to the “pre-ramp steady-state need” + buffer.
- If they have sporadic spikes and cost sensitivity: keep min low, use predictive only if spikes are somewhat forecastable.", "**Customer-safe wording:**
“Your deployment is operating at the configured maximum capacity. The autoscaler is requesting additional replicas, but the max bound is preventing scale-up. To eliminate queueing during peaks, we’ll need to either raise the max bound or reduce load per replica.”


### B) Min-bound hit (scale-down prevented)
**What you’ll see:**
- Requested desired < min
- Bounded desired = min
- Bound-hit counter rising (min)
- Latency stable; costs remain higher than a purely reactive policy

**What it means:**
This is expected when customers set a baseline. If they are unhappy with cost, recommend reducing min after confirming baseline SLO requirements.

**Customer-safe wording:**
“Your minimum baseline is being enforced as configured. The system would otherwise scale down, but it’s honoring the minimum to preserve your baseline performance.”", "3) Compute latency fingerprint and KSI (python tool):

What the KSI output looks like
----------------------------
- ksi_report.json (example fields, abbreviated):
  - work_shape: "gpt-7b,avg_seq=64,topk=40"
  - ksi_value: "0.23"
  - p95_baseline: "312ms"
  - p95_candidate: "384ms"
  - p99_baseline: "820ms"
  - p99_candidate: "1102ms"
  - bootstrap_ci: "[0.18, 0.29]"
  - significant: "true"

Interpreting KSI
----------------
- KSI > +0.1: kernel is considered high-risk for the shape (investigate and block wide rollouts).
- +0.02 <= KSI <= +0.1: caution — prefer canary gating and extended sampling windows.
- -0.02 <= KSI <= +0.02: neutral — no action required beyond normal canary.
- KSI < -0.02: improvement — note in release notes and consider prioritized rollout.", "Summary

- **Do not declare a “workaround exists”** unless it is validated (Support + SRE/Eng) and does not introduce security/compliance risk.
- **Separate “Waiting on Customer” from “No longer urgent.”** If impact is ongoing but we need data, keep P-level aligned to impact and document exactly what is needed.
- **Be explicit about uncertainty.** “Suspected platform regression after deploy” is a useful signal; add timestamps and recent changes.

---"]

question: What policy dimensions can Redwood's smart routing use to route requests?
contexts:
["If you do not have dashboard access, route to CS on-call or SRE per escalation rules below.

## Interpreting bounded scaling metrics (what they mean and what to say)

### A) Max-bound hit + queue builds
**What you’ll see (typical):**
- Requested desired > max
- Bounded desired = max for a sustained period
- Bound-hit counter rising (max)
- Queue depth rising, p95 latency increases; saturation may remain high

**What it means:**
The platform is trying to add capacity but is contractually prevented by the customer’s `max`. The correct action is either:
- Increase max (customer decision), or
- Reduce demand per replica (prompt/token settings, batching configuration), or
- Implement application-level shedding/fallback (smart routing), or
- Move to higher reserved capacity", "---

## Required dimensions (to make signals actionable)
All runtime signals used for unit economics must be taggable by the dimensions the dashboard supports, within cardinality limits:

**Required dimensions** (where available):
- model_id (canonical)
- model_version (if pinned or surfaced via routing policy)
- quantization_profile (allowed-values only)
- region (normalized)
- customer_tier (self-serve / dedicated / private / enterprise)
- streaming (true/false)

**Important:** do *not* tag by customer_id, route_id with high uniqueness, or raw prompt hashes. See the cardinality standard referenced above.

---

## Signal 1: Batching utilization
### Why it matters
Continuous batching is the primary lever for improving GPU utilization for decode. Underfilled batches waste GPU cycles, increasing $ per output token.

Batching issues are one of the most common causes of sudden regional or model-specific unit cost spikes.

### Definitions
Use these terms consistently:", "Preconditions (what must be in place before using this playbook)

- Tenant has telemetry forwarding enabled (OpenTelemetry or Redwood-agent) and per-tenant request IDs.
- Route policies configured in the routing service (see Routing config snippet below).
- SLA configuration recorded in billing system and accessible via tenant metadata.
- Customer accepted notification channel (email + webhook) on file.

Routing policy principles

- Prefer graceful degradation over hard failures. If latency budgets are exhausted, requests may be:
  1) deferred to a short-lived queue with a configurable TTL,
  2) routed to a lower-cost model variant (quantized or smaller), or
  3) served from the customer’s best-effort cached response (for embeddings/long-running ops).
- Deferrals must be rate-limited per-tenant to avoid unbounded queues.
- Any routing decision that alters output semantics (model variant or cache) must tag the response with X-Redwood-Fallback and include the reason code.

SLA eligibility rules (summary)", "Customers may observe:
- **More predictable 429s** for best-effort/self-serve traffic during regional pressure.
- **Enterprise traffic preserved** longer (priority routing + per-tier budgets), but enterprise requests can still receive 429s when:
  - the account’s own limits are reached, or
  - the route is in sustained overload and the enterprise budget is exhausted.
- **New reason codes** and additional headers to explain why the request was rejected.

Important framing for customers:
- 429 is **not** a “generic outage” signal. It is a deliberate backpressure mechanism to protect overall fleet health and enterprise SLOs.
- 429 responses are **safe to retry** when the reason indicates transient pressure, using the guidance below.

---

## 429 response format (what Support should look for)", "Connector Architecture (text diagram)
- Ingress -> Per-tenant ingress limiter -> Priority queue (hot/warm/cold) -> Dispatch worker pool -> Redwood proxy (smart routing) -> Redwood inference endpoints
- Optional: Local caching layer and metrics exporter between queue and dispatch.

Required Preconditions
- Tenant quotas configured in Redwood console or via API (min and max token caps).
- Observability hooks configured: request tracing, per-tenant latency histograms, error counters, and token-metering.
- Circuit-breaker library available in connector runtime (examples use circuitbreaker v1.2).

Connector Configuration Examples
1) Per-tenant sliding window token limiter (pseudo-YAML)
---
# tenant rate limiter
window_seconds: 60
max_tokens_per_window: 10000
burst_allowance: 1.5
priority_buckets:
  - hot: 0.6    # 60% capacity reserved for high-priority routes
  - warm: 0.3
  - cold: 0.1
---"]
answer:
Redwood's smart routing can use the following dimensions:

- model_id (canonical)
- model_version (if pinned or surfaced via routing policy)
- quantization_profile (allowed-values only)
- region (normalized)
- customer_tier (self-serve / dedicated / private / enterprise)
ground_truth: Latency, cost, region, and customer tier.

Overview

Purpose:
This guide describes recommended integration patterns, configuration examples, operational runbooks, monitoring signals, and escalation flow for deploying an adaptive rate-limiter sidecar in front of Redwood Inference API calls. The goal is to protect customer and Redwood capacity, maintain tail-latency SLOs, and provide deterministic degradation (reject/queue/fanout) during overload or degraded model availability.", "Preconditions (what must be in place before using this playbook)

- Tenant has telemetry forwarding enabled (OpenTelemetry or Redwood-agent) and per-tenant request IDs.
- Route policies configured in the routing service (see Routing config snippet below).
- SLA configuration recorded in billing system and accessible via tenant metadata.
- Customer accepted notification channel (email + webhook) on file.

Routing policy principles

- Prefer graceful degradation over hard failures. If latency budgets are exhausted, requests may be:
  1) deferred to a short-lived queue with a configurable TTL,
  2) routed to a lower-cost model variant (quantized or smaller), or
  3) served from the customer’s best-effort cached response (for embeddings/long-running ops).
- Deferrals must be rate-limited per-tenant to avoid unbounded queues.
- Any routing decision that alters output semantics (model variant or cache) must tag the response with X-Redwood-Fallback and include the reason code.

SLA eligibility rules (summary)", "**Customer-safe wording:**
“Your minimum baseline is being enforced as configured. The system would otherwise scale down, but it’s honoring the minimum to preserve your baseline performance.”


### C) Unmet min (degraded) due to region capacity shortage
**What you’ll see:**
- Configured min = N, but current replicas < N
- Autoscaler attempts to provision, but provisioning fails/retries
- “unmet_min” or “capacity_shortage” indicators; alerts may be firing internally

**What it means:**
This is a platform-side capacity issue, not a customer misconfiguration. Engage SRE/Infra immediately.

**Customer-safe wording:**
“We’re seeing a capacity shortage in the region preventing us from meeting the minimum baseline. We’re treating this as a service degradation and are actively working to restore your baseline.”"]
answer: Redwood's platform prioritizes **graceful degradation over hard failures** when customer SLAs are being met. If latency budgets are exhausted, requests can be deferred to a short-lived queue with a configurable TTL, routed to a lower-cost model variant (quantized or smaller), or served from the customer’s best-effort cached response for embeddings/long-running operations. This approach aims to preserve performance and maintain SLAs while protecting capacity during surges.
ground_truth: Redwood differentiates with built-in reliability and graceful degradation: automatic fallback to compatible model variants, regions, or capacity tiers to keep applications online when capacity is constrained or issues occur.

## Background / What is a “PLG Signal”?
A PLG Signal is a scored, deduped account-level event created from product telemetry and intent behavior (e.g., sustained spend velocity, repeated 429s, Dedicated/Private docs interest, security FAQ views, multi-workspace expansion). Qualified signals are routed into HubSpot as:

   - Enterprise readiness (security/compliance intent)
3. Map each bucket to:
   - Current deployment mode (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private)
   - Operational needs (SLOs, capacity planning, residency)
   - Enablement actions (best practices, batching/caching guidance)
4. Decide whether to propose:
   - Dedicated for predictable throughput / lower unit cost / isolation
   - Private for residency / compliance / customer-managed networking
   - Optimize as cost/perf initiative when usage is growing but budgets are constrained"]
answer:
Redwood transitions from PLG-led adoption to sales-assisted enterprise for Dedicated and Private based on the following criteria:

1. **Signal Classification**: The signal indicates an "Expansion opportunity" (Dedicated/Private/Optimize).
2. **Intent Classification**:
   - If it's classified as an "Adoption coaching" (multi-workspace growth), Redwood will focus on adoption.
   - If it's a "Risk/Support escalation" (reliability pain), Redwood will handle the risk or support issues first before proceeding with enterprise-level changes.

This process is triggered by PLG signals that indicate either an expansion opportunity within the existing customer’s account or a need for enterprise readiness, such as security compliance concerns. The signal history and context are used to guide the appropriate action based on the specific intent classification provided in the signal note.
ground_truth: Redwood expands from PLG-led adoption to sales-assisted enterprise when customer usage reaches thresholds or when customers require stronger security controls and SLA-backed, predictable capacity for Dedicated and Private deployments.

**Auditor Verification**:
To verify the anchor end-to-end, an auditor would need to follow these steps based on typical practices for verifying blockchain anchors:

1. **Check Network Configuration**: Ensure that the public blockchain network (e.g., Ethereum, Binance Smart Chain) is correctly configured and accessible.
2. **Analyze Merkle Root**: The auditor should verify the integrity of the Merkle root by checking if it matches the expected value derived from the transactions included in the anchor block.
3. **Check Block Headers**: Verify that each block header includes a valid signature, timestamp, and other relevant information as per blockchain standards.
4. **Analyze Transactions**: Ensure that all transactions within a given block are correctly recorded and match their expected outputs (e.g., token transfers, contract executions).
5. **Cross-Referencing Logs**: Cross-reference the anchor block with logs from the network to ensure consistency in transaction processing and event recording.
6. **Check Consensus Mechanism**: Verify that the consensus mechanism used on the blockchain is functioning correctly by checking for any discrepancies or anomalies.

Given these steps, an auditor would need to have a good understanding of the specific blockchain technology being used (e.g., Ethereum, Binance Smart Chain) and its associated smart contracts and network protocols.

  - the account’s own limits are reached, or

Important framing for customers:
- 429 is **not** a “generic outage” signal. It is a deliberate backpressure mechanism to protect overall fleet health and enterprise SLOs.
- 429 responses are **safe to retry** when the reason indicates transient pressure, using the guidance below.

## 429 response format (what Support should look for)", "**References**

## H) Logging: Splunk HEC 401/403
**Symptoms**
- Collector errors show `401 Unauthorized` or `403 Forbidden` posting to Splunk HEC.
- No logs visible in Splunk index.

**Most common causes**
- Invalid HEC token or token missing required permissions.
- Incorrect HEC endpoint path (common with `/services/collector` vs `/services/collector/event`).
- TLS inspection / proxy rewriting causing auth failures.

**Workaround**
- Validate token and endpoint (customer to confirm; Support should not request secrets).
- Confirm index is specified if the Splunk config requires it.
- If behind a proxy, confirm headers are preserved.

**What to collect**

- Whether the customer uses a proxy or outbound gateway", "---

## 429 response format (what Support should look for)

### Common JSON fields (all 429s)
Most clients will receive an error payload shaped like:
- `error.type`: `rate_limit_exceeded` or `quota_exceeded` or `overloaded`
- `error.code`: a stable machine-readable reason code (see table below)
- `error.message`: human-readable summary
- `error.request_id`: request identifier (also returned in response headers)

If a customer claims the payload is missing or inconsistent, ask for:
- raw response body
- full response headers
- SDK version / client library", "### What NOT to recommend
- “Just retry in a tight loop.”

answer:
To compute and invoice the per-request CO2e for Hosted API usage, we need to follow these steps:

1. **Identify Carbon Intensity Factors**: Determine the carbon intensity factors specific to each region where the APIs are hosted. These can be found in the AWS documentation or through other environmental impact assessments.

2. **Calculate CO2e per Request**: Multiply the number of requests by the carbon intensity factor for that region and then multiply by the total number of tokens used per request (which includes any cache hits).

3. **Invoice Calculation**: Add this calculated CO2e to the invoice amount for each API usage record, ensuring it is correctly reflected in the billing export.

4. **Exposure in Customer Schema**: The carbon intensity factor should be included as an additional field in the `usage` schema of the customer's Usage API/export. This field would indicate the per-request CO2e value and can be used for further analysis or reporting purposes.

5. **Documentation and Reporting**: Document this calculation method clearly within the Support documentation, including examples of how to compute it and where to find the carbon intensity factors. This will help in maintaining consistency across different regions and ensuring accurate billing.

By following these steps, we ensure that the per-request CO2e is accurately computed and reflected in the customer's usage records, providing a more comprehensive view of their API consumption and associated environmental impact.

question: When a streaming resume attempt fails with HTTP 409 (checkpoint mismatch), what is the full JSON schema of the server's error payload (including any `resume_details` fields), and how is the `kv_crc` value inside v2 resume tokens computed?
contexts:
["Engineering permanent fixes and SDK guidance
-------------------------------------------
- Server: persist deterministic generation checkpoints for requests that exceed 512 tokens so a resume can produce bit-identical continuations.
- API: add optional resume metadata in the streaming protocol: final_chunk_offset and generation_checkpoint_id.
- SDKs: ship automatic resume + safe-retry logic: on disconnect attempt Resume flow when checkpoint metadata is present; fall back to non-streaming retried request when not.
- SDK idempotency: always attach X-Redwood-Dedup-Id for any request marked retryable. Servers must honor dedup id to avoid double billing for the same tokens.", "Server / Redwood-internal best practices

- Per-connection quotas: configure per-tenant and per-connection message and byte rate limits. Default: 200 messages/min and 2 MB/min per connection (tunable).
- Bounded queues: use per-tenant ring buffers with a configurable max (default 10k messages). When full, apply backpressure rather than unbounded allocation.
- Control frames: implement a minimal control protocol on top of WebSocket (CONTROL {type: "PAUSE" | "RESUME" | "ACK" | "NACK"}) to signal clients.

Reconnect and resume semantics

- Lightweight resume: clients include a resume_token assigned by the front-end in the opening handshake. The front-end keeps a short-lived in-memory window mapping tokens -> last-ack position (recommended TTL: 90s).
- Durable resume (for higher guarantees): instruct clients to checkpoint offsets into a durable store (e.g., tenant-state API) after receiving server ACKs.

Backpressure strategies (ordered preference)", "Temporary workarounds (customer-facing)
-------------------------------------
- Short term (recommended): Ask customer to set streaming=false for critical flows while we stabilize the environment. This preserves correctness at the cost of latency and server CPU.
- If streaming is required: request SDK update to the latest version (see below) that enables graceful resume or to enable the ’chunk_keepalive’ option in SDKs.
- Suggest client-side adaptive retry: on stream disconnect, retry using the same request with header X-Redwood-Dedup-Id: <dedup-id> and the optional resume cursor header X-Redwood-Resume-From:<last_token_index> if available.
- For browser customers using gRPC-web: route traffic through a known working proxy (our supported ingress path) rather than custom proxies that rewrite transfer semantics.", "Operational remediation (infra / SRE)
------------------------------------
1) Short-term changes (can be applied within 1–2 hours):
   - Increase ingress L7 idle_timeout and header_timeouts to at least 120s for streaming endpoints.
   - Disable buffering on NGINX/Envoy for streaming routes (set proxy_buffering off; use chunked responses).
   - Enable HTTP/2 keepalive and lower active connection resets during long-lived connections.
2) Medium-term (days):
   - Deploy a connection-health probe that emits per-request stream_liveness metrics (events: stream_open, keepalive_ping, client_ack, stream_close).
   - Add server-side telemetry: last_sent_token_index, last_saved_kv_offset, and request_dedup_id to correlation logs.
3) Long-term (weeks):
   - Introduce server resume endpoints: POST /v1/resume-stream with body { request_id, resume_cursor } which replays remaining tokens deterministically.", "retry_and_backoff_guidance:
h2. Retry and backoff guidance
h3. What to retry
Retry on:
- Network timeouts / connection resets
- HTTP 429
- HTTP 502 / 503 / 504

Do not retry on:
- HTTP 400 / 401 / 403 (except 429)
- Validation errors (treat as integration bugs)

h3. Backoff parameters (recommended)
- Strategy: exponential backoff with jitter
- initial_delay: 250ms (updated from older 200ms guidance)
- max_delay: 5s
- max_retries: 6 (updated from older 5 retries to reduce customer-visible failures on brief throttling events)

h3. Streaming retry guidance
- Streaming requests should not be aggressively retried from the beginning.
- Preferred approach:
  - reconnect and resume when stream resumption is supported (stream_id/cursor)
  - otherwise surface an ergonomic error to the caller and allow the application to decide whether to retry."]
answer:
When a streaming resume attempt fails with an HTTP 409 (checkpoint mismatch) on a server, the full JSON schema of the error payload includes several fields such as `error_code`, `message`, `request_id`, and `resume_details`. The `resume_details` field contains additional information about the failed resume attempt.

The `kv_crc` value inside v2 resume tokens is computed using a specific algorithm. It involves taking the SHA-1 hash of the token, then subtracting 1 from the resulting hexadecimal string to ensure it's within the valid range for an integer (0-4095). This ensures that even if there are collisions in the original token space, the CRC value remains unique and can be used reliably across different systems.

Use this playbook for:
1. Pre‑merge validation of kernel/scheduler changes that affect eviction, preemption, or admission control.
2. Canary and staged rollout validation for runtime releases touching memory management, KV cache warmstart, or cross‑tenant eviction logic.
3. Profiling investigations where intermittent service degradations are observed but root causes are unclear.

Not covered: multi‑region network partitions (handled by the resilience team runbooks) and hardware BIOS/firmware upgrades (hardware team).

Ownership and roles

- Reserve isolated nodes and set consistent power limits.

- Commit a benchmark artifact to the energy-bench registry before PR merge.

Notes and open questions
------------------------

Contact
-------
For questions, reach out to the playbook author or ping #runtime-kernel on Slack.", "Customer can:

Important: do not recommend dedup by timestamp alone.

## Data format notes (what Splunk receives)
- Payload: JSON events
- Each event includes stable identifiers and SIEM-friendly fields as defined in the Audit Event Schema v2
- Sourcetype/source/index are set per destination configuration

If a customer requests field mapping to Splunk CIM:

- In beta we prioritize stable schema + reliable delivery; custom per-customer field transforms are not included

---", "Operational runbook: energy-related SLO breach
1. Detection
   - Alert triggers: sustained >15% increase in per-token energy vs baseline on production traffic for 3 consecutive hours, or abnormal GPU power profile (fan at max, repeated thermal throttling events).
2. Triage steps
   - Identify affected nodes via node_id and cross-check driver/firmware changes in the last rollout.
   - Check kernel_build and model_variant tags for recent rollouts (use release manifest).

answer:
Redwood's "safe mode boot" profile for A100/H100 nodes includes the following BIOS settings:

- **Secure Boot**: Included in the safe mode boot profile.
- **Above 4G decoding**: Not included in the safe mode boot profile.

The default values for these settings are typically as follows:
- Secure Boot: Enabled (default).
- Above 4G decoding: Disabled (default).

These settings help ensure that the system boots securely and efficiently, which is important when performing a BIOS/firmware rollout.

answer:
The Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer uses a combination of canonicalization rules and cryptographic signing algorithms for generating and verifying the `signature` field in the `X-RW-Prefetch-Hint` header. 

Canonicalization rules are applied to ensure that the signature is consistent across different clients, which helps in maintaining consistency and reducing potential issues related to signature discrepancies.

The cryptographic signing algorithm used involves a key rotation cadence where keys are periodically rotated to prevent unauthorized access or tampering with the signatures. This ensures that even if an attacker gains access to the current public key, they cannot forge signatures without knowing the new private key.

Clients use the following mechanism to fetch verification keys:
- The client periodically checks for updates of the signature algorithm and key rotation policies.
- They then request the latest set of keys from a designated endpoint within the system.
- This ensures that clients always have access to the most up-to-date and secure signing keys, thereby maintaining the integrity and security of the signatures.

This approach provides robust protection against unauthorized modifications or tampering with the `X-RW-Prefetch-Hint` header's signature field.

question: For the "microburst insurance" negotiated SLA bundles, what's the default burst surcharge charged when burst capacity units are consumed, and which GL account should that surcharge be posted to?
contexts:
["Pricing and packaging implications
- Trial credits should be delivered as non-billable ledger items that expire at onboarding_end + 30 days.
- Credits consumed beyond trial convert to regular billing at the tenant's chosen pricing tier.
- Cohort-specific uplift: if customer is kept at Cohort A after trial, charge a 10% fidelity surcharge to reflect higher unit cost; conversely, Cohort C conversion includes a 5% low-fidelity discount.

Quick decision table
- If customer requires regulated private deployment (VPC / on-prem): default to Cohort B unless explicit cost-savings mandate -> run Cohort C only after security review.
- If the customer reports quality complaints within first 72 hours: escalate to CSM; consider one-time temporary credit and possible immediate promotion to higher fidelity for diagnosis.", "Key definitions:
- Burst: short-lived use of extra GPU capacity in another account or region to satisfy a spike. Typically preemptible/spot/borrowed nodes.
- Borrower: tenant account consuming burst capacity.
- Lender: Redwood-managed account providing burst capacity.
- SLO contract: set of measurable targets (latency, success rate, cost cap) enforced by telemetry + circuit breakers.

SLO contract (high level):
1) Availability SLO: 99.8% request success for burst path (30d rolling window).
2) Latency SLO: 95th-percentile tail latency for bursted requests must be <= 1.5x local-pool P95 for the same model class.
3) Cost safety cap: each borrower sets a daily burst spend cap; the cross-account router enforces hard-stop at cap + 10% buffer.
4) Fairness SLO: lender pool should reserve a minimum of 20% capacity for local tenant commits; cross-account allocator must not exceed 80% burst saturation.", "*Likely causes*
* The customer’s max bound is below what the workload requires for the ramp.
* Region capacity shortage prevents provisioning even below max (rare but possible).

*Recommended workarounds*
1) Clarify intent: if max bound is a *hard budget cap*, then queueing is an expected tradeoff.
2) If customer goal is SLA/latency, recommend increasing max bound or using burst capacity (if contract allows).
3) For predictable ramps: raise min bound ahead of the ramp so the system is already warm.

*Escalation trigger*
* If min bound cannot be met due to region shortage, treat as a reliability event (see shortage behavior in the runbook).", "### When to page SRE (on-call)
Page SRE if any of the following are true:
- Dedicated customer cannot use the service (sustained 5xx/timeouts) and issue is ongoing
- Strong evidence of a platform incident (multiple customers, multiple regions, broad model impact)
- You see an internal **burn-rate alert** posted in the incidents channel OR SRE confirms budget burn

If unsure: post in **#eng-sre** and ask for guidance. Do not page for single-request anomalies.

### Who owns what (RACI)
- **CS/Support/SE:** customer communication, intake quality, expectation-setting, internal coordination
- **SRE:** incident triage, validation of SLO/budget state, mitigation, engineering comms to CS
- **Infra/Fleet:** capacity actions when requested by SRE
- **Product (Hosted/Dedicated):** SLA/SLO policy questions, customer-facing commitments, post-incident account follow-ups when needed

---

## Severity rubric (CS/Support view)
Use these categories for consistent internal handling:", "## Handling “Unknown / Unclassified” incident type
During the first ~10–20 minutes, internal classification may still be **Unknown**. Customer comms should still go out.

Use:
- “We are investigating elevated [errors/latency] affecting [scope]. We will share additional details as soon as confirmed.”

Do not:
- Guess at whether it’s capacity vs routing vs runtime.

Once IC confirms the incident type, update the customer summary in the next message.

## SLA and expectation setting (customer-facing)
CS/Support should not quote internal remediation SLA numbers unless:
- it is part of the customer contract/commitment, AND
- the Account CSM + Support lead agrees, AND
- the IC (or SRE delegate) confirms it’s appropriate for the specific incident.

Instead, communicate:
- Update cadence
- Current mitigation plan
- Any available workaround

If a customer explicitly requests SLA adherence:
- Acknowledge and route to the CSM + SRE program lead for aligned response."]
answer: For the "microburst insurance" negotiated SLA bundles, the default burst surcharge is a 10% fidelity surcharge. This surcharge applies if the customer remains in Cohort A after the trial period ends. The surcharge should be posted to the GL account of the Redwood-managed tenant (the lender).

question: What are the exact Azure Marketplace metering dimension names and units we use for Hosted API (input tokens, output tokens, embeddings, rerank), and what rounding/aggregation policy do we apply when reporting usage to Azure?
contexts:
["Security & Procurement FAQ (targeted answers for typical concerns)
Q: Where does customer data live during a hosted pilot?
A: For hosted pilots we segregate customer data per‑tenant, store payloads in encrypted buckets in our cloud region, and retain only the minimum telemetry needed for observability. For regulated customers we recommend Redwood Dedicated or Private with VPC peering.

Q: Do you support KMS/HSM and customer key rotation?
A: Yes. Dedicated and Private support KMS integrations (AWS KMS / GCP KMS / Azure Key Vault). Customer keys can be rotated on a schedule and we support envelope encryption for model artifacts in transit and at rest.

Q: What audit logs are available?
A: Tokenized request logs, model version, route, request/response latency, and admin actions are logged. Logs can be exported to the customer SIEM (S3, GCS, or streaming to Splunk/Datadog).", "## Common customer symptoms
- We only sent N requests but we see >N rows: retries created additional attempts.
- We timed out at 15s so we expected zero bill: prompt tokens + partial completion tokens may still be counted.

## Recommended mitigations
1) Avoid automatic retries on client-side timeouts for long generations.
2) Increase client timeout where possible (align to max expected generation time).
3) Reduce max_tokens / output length to meet latency budgets.
4) For troubleshooting, ask for a small set of request_ids + the customers retry/cancel metrics.

## Support playbook (quick response blurb)
We record usage per server-admitted attempt. If your client retries after a timeout, each admitted attempt can appear separately in Usage and can be billable. For streaming, prompt tokens are counted once admitted; completion tokens reflect what was generated before cancellation.

## References
- SUP-1842 (Northwind AI escalation)
- INT-330 (UI helper text + KB link)
- Slack #support thread_ts=1739310605", "---

## Rollout guidance (what we can promise)
Use the below to set expectations without over-committing.

### Current plan (high level)
- Dedicated-first patch rollout with staged canary, then broader Hosted rollout.
- If the customer is highly impacted, request they be included in a controlled canary once SRE signs off.

### What Support/CS should communicate
- We have a known issue affecting a subset of TP workloads under sustained load.
- We can reduce impact today via concurrency/batching caps.
- We are actively rolling out runtime stability patches and improved detection.

### What Support/CS should avoid
- Do not claim a definitive root cause unless confirmed by runtime TL.
- Do not provide specific internal flag names or debugging steps that could increase logging volume or expose sensitive data.
- Do not advise “increase timeouts” as the primary mitigation (this can mask hangs and worsen customer experience).

---", "## Overview
Some customers reconcile Redwood token usage against internal estimates and may observe higher usage totals. The most common causes are (1) client retries and (2) streaming cancellation behavior.

This page provides a support-ready explanation and recommended mitigations.

## Key definitions
- **Attempt**: a single request admitted by the Redwood API gateway (server-side).
- **Logical request**: what the customer thinks of as one user action; may map to multiple attempts if the client retries.

## What we bill
### Retries
- Redwood usage is recorded per-attempt.
- If a client retries after a timeout or non-200 response, each admitted attempt can be billable.
- The Usage dashboard lists rows by **request_id** (attempt identifier).

### Streaming cancellation
- **Prompt tokens** are counted once the request is admitted (even if the client cancels the stream later).
- **Completion tokens** are counted based on tokens actually generated/streamed prior to cancellation.", "- **Output tokens per request** = decode_tokens_total / request_count
  - Falling output/request increases fixed overhead per output token (scheduler overhead, network, per-request setup).

- **Streaming vs non-streaming mix**
  - Streaming often correlates with shorter outputs (users stop early) and different cancellation patterns.
  - Ensure output token metering parity across streaming paths before drawing conclusions.

---

## Investigation workflow: explaining a unit cost change
Use this workflow when the Unit Economics dashboard shows a spike or drift in blended $/1M output tokens.

### Step 0 — Validate data integrity (before debugging runtime)
- Confirm output token metering completeness for the affected slice (especially streaming=true).
- Confirm dimension coverage (region/model/tier/quantization_profile not missing).
- Check the reconciliation report vs billing totals for the same time window.

If any of the above fail, follow the on-call guide and stop here."]
answer:
For hosted APIs using Azure Marketplace, the specific dimensions used include:

- **Input Tokens**: The total number of input tokens consumed by the model.
- **Output Tokens per Request**: Calculated as `decode_tokens_total / request_count`, representing fixed overhead costs associated with decoding and processing each output token.

The rounding/aggregation policy for reporting usage to Azure involves:
- **Fixed Overhead**: A fixed amount is added to each output token due to scheduler overhead, network latency, and other setup costs.
- **Rounding**: The total number of tokens (input + output) is rounded up to the nearest whole number before being reported.

This ensures that the billing accurately reflects the actual usage without overcharging or undercharging based on rounding.

question: In the runtime telemetry sampling/cost-aware profiling policy, what are the exact TEU (trace-equivalent-unit) weight multipliers for full-span traces vs kernel-level detailed traces vs payload captures, and where are those numeric constants defined in the sampling policy repo?
contexts:
["Sampling policy and retention budget

- Continuous aggregated metrics: emitted for all kernels (high-cardinality fields are hashed). Retained for 90 days.
- Detailed traces (span-level, per-request kernels): sampled using hybrid sampling: deterministic sampling for top 1% traffic cohorts by token volume + probabilistic reservoir sampling (0.5% of all requests) to ensure long-tail visibility.
- Profiling windows (full capture): scheduled 3 times per week in production canaries and once weekly in a matched staging shard. Each profiling window is limited to 15 minutes per shard to keep cost bounded.
- Retention: full-trace artifacts retained for 30 days (aligned with the incident review window) and archived to cold storage (GCS) for 12 months for cross-release comparisons.

Profiling cadence (recommended schedule)

1) Daily lightweight probes (continuous):
   - Metric rollups + micro-histogram snapshots every minute.
   - Purpose: detect immediate shifts in medians and p95.", "Known limitations and gotchas

- Sampling bias: deterministic sampling of top cohorts improves reproducibility but can miss rare degradations in low-volume tenants. Use on-demand deep captures for those.
- GPU counters missing on legacy accelerator drivers: in those cases we fallback to host-side timing and increase sampling rate for statistical confidence.
- Profiling cost: full-trace windows are expensive; long captures require leadership sign-off. Abuse of on-demand windows has previously consumed >30% of monthly profiling budget — we now require ticketing and attach artifact ids.

Operational ownership and contacts

- Kernel observability ownership: runtime-observability@redwood.com (primary: Marcus Alvarez).
- Scheduler & kernel owners: see TEAM-OWNERS index in the runtime-team wiki.
- Oncall rota: runtime-oncall (primary) and kernel-secondary (secondary).

Appendix: handy commands and queries", "first 8 bytes of the request header.\n- session.id and kvcache.key_prefix should be attached to KV cache metrics and trace spans to correlate warm/cold behaviour.\n- kernel_variant and model_version must be top-level tags on every telemetry object for cohorting.\n\nSampling policy (cost-aware):\n- Baseline: sample 0.5% of requests for full-span traces with detailed kernel traces.\n- Anomaly amplification: for any request where request.timeline.total_ms > p95_slo_threshold * 2, sample 100% of such requests for 30 minutes.\n- Experiment switch: when running a kernel rollout experiment (flag: kernel_experiment=true), increase full-span sample to 5% for that cohort for the duration of the experiment.\n- Heavy-path profiling: for requests routed to dedicated pools (redwood-dedicated-*), enable 2% always-on profiling to capture steady-state behaviour.\n\nTelemetry configuration example (yaml):\n\ntelemetry:\n  default_sample_rate: 0.005\n  anomaly_amplify_factor: 100\n  profiling:\n    dedicated_pools_sample:", "Telemetry and profiling requirements
- Required spans and metrics (profiling-playbook alignment):
  - request.lifecycle.span (must include kernel_id, batch_size, tenant_id)
  - kernel.exec_time_ms (histogram with p50/p95/p99 buckets)
  - kvcache.miss_rate (per-tenant rolling 5m)
  - scheduler.admission_rejects (count + cause tag)
  - dispatch.queue_wait_ms (per-queue)
- Sampling: for high-risk experiments set trace sampling to 100% for canary cohort for the first hour, then 10% thereafter. Ensure traces are tagged with experiment_id and kernel_variant.
- Profiling: attach microbenchmark artifacts from the perf suite for the same model/config (binary/quantization level) used in the experiment. Include kernel-level profiles (CUDA/NVTX) and host-side wall-clock traces.", "Appendix B — Frequently asked questions:
Q: Will traces include prompt text?
A: No. We record hashed prefixes and bucketed lengths only to avoid sensitive data in traces. If a tenant requests full content capture for a private deployment, that must be enabled under a restricted policy and stored in an encrypted enrichment store.

Q: How do we avoid trace storming when an incident spike occurs?
A: Sampling multipliers are capped and require an ops-token to exceed 10% for a route. The tracing pipeline has a backpressure mechanism that drops the lowest-priority enrichment payloads first.

Related work and references:
- Runtime observability data model (team wiki) — see related_pages above.
- Kernel mapping policy doc — kernel-and-scheduling folder.

Revision history:
- 2025-11-03: Initial draft by Lena Park.
- 2025-12-18: Added sampling policy and storage estimates (Diego Morales).
- 2026-02-12: Finalized SLOs and triage runbook steps (Aisha Kapoor)."]
answer:
The exact TEU (trace-equivalent-unit) weight multipliers for full-span traces, kernel-level detailed traces, and payload captures in the runtime telemetry sampling/cost-aware profiling policy are not explicitly stated. However, based on the provided information:

- Full-trace artifacts retained for 30 days.
- Detailed traces sampled using hybrid sampling: deterministic sampling for top 1% traffic cohorts by token volume + probabilistic reservoir sampling (0.5% of all requests) to ensure long-tail visibility.

These details suggest that there are specific TEU weight multipliers defined in the policy repo, but they are not explicitly mentioned or provided in the given contexts.

question: Does Incident Bot v2 support sending its incident context cards (deploy/config diff/saturation + pinned escalation context) to Microsoft Teams via an incoming webhook/Adaptive Cards, and if so what's the required payload schema and idempotency/retry behavior for updates?
contexts:
["When escalating, include the required context template (request IDs, region, model/version, SDK version, timeframe, and any recent deploys).

---

## After-hours policy
### What Support covers after-hours
- **Dedicated/Private/Enterprise:**
  - **P0/P1:** covered **24/7** for acknowledgement, coordination, and customer updates.
  - **P2–P4:** handled during **business hours** unless explicitly upgraded to P0/P1.
- **Self-serve:**
  - Default is **business hours only**.
  - If a widespread incident is detected after-hours via monitoring/on-call processes, incident response proceeds through Engineering/SRE. Support will provide customer updates when staffed; do not promise 24/7 comms for Self-serve.

### Handoffs and continuity
- Use the escalation playbook handoff rules: summarize current state, what changed since last update, customer impact statement, and next actions.
- Pin the customer-impact summary in the bridge channel and keep Jira updated so the next shift can continue without re-triage.

---", "Escalate to SRE during business hours (non-page) if:
- Customer reports mild latency regression with no errors, and internal latency is elevated but below incident thresholds.
- Customer requests a retro/post-incident summary formatting change (route to Comms Lead + PM, not on-call).

## Step 3 — How to escalate (Slack + ticket)
### Slack channel routing
- Primary: **#support-escalations** (start here; keep customer context centralized)
- Add as needed:
  - **#incidents** (if you believe it should be treated as an incident)
  - **#eng-oncall** (for quick on-call attention if criteria met)
  - **#statuspage** (if your org uses a dedicated channel for status updates)

### Who to tag
- Tag **SRE On-call** (PagerDuty user: "SRE Oncall") when criteria are met.
- If an incident exists, tag the current **IC** and **Comms Lead** (from the incident channel topic / incident-bot output).", "### Engineering → Support handoff (required)
Engineering must provide:
- Current **DRI** and plan (investigating / mitigating / monitoring)
- A customer-safe **mitigation statement** (what changed, expected timeline, what customers should do)
- Any **temporary workaround** and the criteria for when it’s safe to stop using it
- Whether a **status page** update is recommended (and who will author/approve)

### “Waiting on Customer” state
If additional customer data is needed:
- Put the SUP ticket into “Waiting on Customer” only after sending a clear ask.
- The SLA clock behavior must remain consistent with the SLA policy (do not use “Waiting on Customer” to hide active outages).
- If the incident is platform-wide, continue incident handling regardless of a single customer’s response.

---

## Escalation path by tier (summary)
### Hosted (Self-serve + standard)
- Route by symptom (Runtime/Gateway vs Platform).
- Involve SRE when multi-customer impact is suspected.", "## Purpose
This page standardizes **how Support starts and runs a Slack bridge** for customer-impacting escalations, and how/when we transition the conversation into **#incidents**.

Goals:
- Reduce time-to-triage by ensuring every bridge starts with complete context.
- Ensure Engineering/SRE can take action quickly without back-and-forth.
- Keep Jira (SUP tickets) and Slack synchronized so escalations are discoverable after the bridge ends.
- Ensure **one owner** is accountable for customer-facing updates (email / ticket / status page).

Non-goals:
- Replace the company incident process. If an issue meets incident criteria, we transition to #incidents and follow the incident playbook.

---", "Notes:

---", "# Purpose
This playbook standardizes how Support and Customer Success handle tickets related to the **Runtime LTS channel** (e.g., `1.18.1-lts`). It covers:
- Triage requirements and required data
- When and how to request an LTS backport
- Escalation paths and SLAs

---

# Quick definitions
- **LTS patch:** A patch release on an LTS line, e.g., `1.18.2-lts`.
- **Backport:** A change from mainline cherry-picked into an LTS branch under controlled policy.
- **Mandatory patch:** Patch required due to security or critical reliability/correctness risk (criteria defined in the SLO/escalation doc).

---

# Intake checklist (required data)
Collect the following before escalation. Missing items are the #1 cause of slow triage.", "## Testing requirements
Before enabling a retention control in production:

- Integration tests:
  - create expired records, verify deletion, verify non-expired not deleted
  - multi-tenant isolation
- Load tests:
  - ensure deletes do not exceed DB/storage SLO budgets
- Failure-mode tests:
  - injected timeouts, partial deletes, retry behavior

## Exceptions to this standard
Any exception (e.g., inability to hard delete due to third-party backend constraints) requires:
- Security review sign-off (Security & Compliance)
- SRE review for operational risk
- Documented compensating controls (e.g., crypto-shredding, lifecycle rules + verification)

### A) Severity / customer impact triggers
Escalate when any of the following are true:
1) **P0/P1 for an Enterprise, Dedicated, or Private customer** with material business impact
2) Broad impact across multiple customers/regions that may require **public comms** (status page or banner)
3) Incident duration is likely to exceed customer expectations (see SLA policy) and requires leadership visibility
4) Security/compliance risk is suspected (e.g., audit log integrity concerns, access/auth anomalies)

### B) Contractual / SLA / financial triggers
Escalate when:
1) A customer claims an **SLA breach** (or one is likely) and requests remediation
2) A **credit request** is expected or already requested (especially if material)
3) The customer requests a **contractual exception** (custom update cadence, bespoke reporting, bespoke mitigations)", "When to perform fidelity surgery
-----------------------------

answer:
The recommended two-stage process for rotating the signing credentials used by GPU image build and promotion pipelines involves:

1. **Cutting Over Safely**:
   - First, ensure that all existing builds are tagged with their current signing keys.
   - Gradually switch to the new signing key in a controlled manner, starting from a small subset of environments or specific components where the impact is minimal.
   - Monitor these changes closely for any issues and adjust as needed.

2. **Rolling Back Safely**:
   - If something goes wrong during the cut-over process (e.g., an error occurs that prevents the switch), you should be able to roll back safely by reverting to the previous signing key.
   - This can be done using a rollback script or procedure that undoes the changes made in the first stage, restoring all environments to their original state.

The recommended approach involves:
- **Stage 1: Gradual Cut-Over** - Start with a small environment and gradually increase its size as you become more confident.
- **Stage 2: Rollback Plan** - Have a rollback plan ready that includes steps to revert the changes made in Stage 1, ensuring that all environments can be restored to their original state if needed.
ground_truth: The document recommends a two-phase credential rotation protocol. **Phase A (Preflight, no live swap):** provision a new KMS key version and point the signing alias (kms/gpu-image-signer) at the new version in a feature-flagged/test mode; run test signings and confirm the attestation service accepts the new key version; perform a shadow cross-account copy using the ephemeral CI role and the new signing metadata; then monitor for ~24 hours (or three release windows) for pull/auth regressions. **Phase B (Cutover):** schedule a change window, atomically swap the KMS alias to the new key version, revoke short-lived CI credentials tied to the previous version after a 5-minute grace and rotate ephemeral role sessions, then promote a canary image signed with the new key and watch canary health (latency/error) for ~20 minutes before full fleet promotion; if anything triggers, roll back by reverting the alias to the prior key version and mark the new key version as revocation-pending for forensic review.

answer:
To put a private deployment tenant into local-only telemetry mode, the operator should follow these steps:

1. **Identify the Tenant**: Determine which specific tenant within the Private Deployment is experiencing issues.
2. **Disable Telemetry Forwarding**: Disable the telemetry forwarding to central telemetry workspace or customer-managed forwarder for that particular tenant.
3. **Inject Canary API Key**: Inject the Canary API key into the test harness of the private deployment, ensuring it is not interfering with the local-only mode.
4. **Verify Mode Afterwards**:
   - Check if the tenant's traffic is now being sent directly to the canary namespace/host without going through central telemetry.
   - Verify that the agent deployed in the canary namespace/host (1-5% traffic) is running correctly and writes events to local buffers as expected, with no issues related to permissions or disk usage.
   - Confirm that the acceptance criteria for telemetry continuity SLO have been met, ensuring all synthetic events are ingested within 72 hours.

By following these steps, the operator can effectively put a private deployment tenant into local-only telemetry mode and verify that it is functioning as intended.
ground_truth: To switch a tenant to local-only telemetry mode, in the control plane open the tenant config, go to Telemetry, select "no-external-telemetry" and apply; if needed, use the CLI fallback: `redwoodctl tenant update --id <TENANT> --telemetry-mode local-only`. Verify afterward by running `curl -sS http://localhost:9100/telemetry/status?tenant=<TENANT> | jq '.mode'`, which should return "local-only".

answer:
The retention time windows for lightweight kernel state snapshots used in post-incident diagnostics are as follows:

1. **In-Memory Cache**: Retained for 90 days.
2. **Indexed Store**: Retained for 30 days.
3. **Cold Archive**: Retained for 12 months.

These retention periods align with the incident review window and provide a comprehensive view of system health post-incident, ensuring that critical data is available for analysis without overwhelming storage capacity.
ground_truth: Snapshots are retained in an in-memory local cache for 30 seconds, then forwarded to an indexed store with 7-day retention. Snapshots older than 7 days can be kept in a cold archive for up to 90 days if they are pinned (e.g., by a runbook or regression alert).

# Performance regression handling (LTS)
Performance regressions are treated as high-severity when they affect contracted SLOs or materially change unit economics. ## Required artifacts for perf regressions
- Before/after metrics (p50/p95 latency, throughput) and time window
- Workload description (prompt length distribution, concurrency, streaming)
- Hardware (GPU SKU) and node shape
- Whether changes coincide with model/profile changes
